channel.title: NYT > Arts
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Martin Short and the Secret to Finding Joy While Surviving Tragedy
item.description: The comedy star, who is the subject of a new documentary, has faced a series of unimaginable losses. Yet he says there are always laughs to remember.
item.dc:creator: Jason Zinoman and Thea Traff
item.media:description: Grieving gives you “muscles to survive,” Martin Short said.

item.title: Lisa Ann Walter of ‘Abbott Elementary’ Dishes It Out
item.description: During a midcareer renaissance, the “Abbott Elementary” and “Parent Trap” star is returning to stand-up, propelled by “the insanity of being a grown-ass woman.”
item.dc:creator: Melena Ryzik and Maddy Rotman
item.media:description: “If I think something is funny, I’ll say it, even if maybe I shouldn’t,” Lisa Ann Walter said.

item.title: At Cannes, the Specter of Hollywood Always Looms Large (Even in Its Absence)
item.description: The lack of big studio movies is apparent in the festival’s quiet start, though the filmmakers James Gray and Jane Schoenbrun have made an impression.
item.dc:creator: Manohla Dargis
item.media:description: On hand for the “Fast and the Furious” tribute at Cannes were, from left, Meadow Walker (Paul Walker’s daughter), Jordana Brewster, Vin Diesel, Michelle Rodriguez and Neil H. Moritz.

item.title: Drake Drops 3 Albums: ‘Iceman,’ ‘Habibti’ and ‘Maid of Honour’
item.description: The chart-topping Canadian rapper released an anticipated new album, “Iceman,” on Friday and two previously unannounced LPs, “Habibti” and “Maid of Honour.”
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Abrams
item.media:credit: Emli Bendixen for The New York Times
item.media:description: Between albums, Drake headlined the three-day Wireless Festival in London last summer.

item.title: The Favorites for Eurovision 2026: Finland, Australia, Greece and Others
item.description: Finland is the favorite for prediction markets and bookmakers, but singers from Australia, Denmark, Greece and Israel are coming for the title, too.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall

item.title: Review: Frida Kahlo and Diego Rivera, Reunited in Opera
item.description: “El Último Sueño de Frida y Diego,” Gabriela Lena Frank and Nilo Cruz’s fantasy about two titans of Mexican art, arrives at the Metropolitan Opera.
item.dc:creator: Joshua Barone
item.category: El Ultimo Sueno de Frida y Diego (Opera)
item.media:description: Carlos Álvarez, left, and Isabel Leonard as Diego Rivera and Frida Kahlo in “El Último Sueño de Frida y Diego” at the Metropolitan Opera.

item.title: Smithsonian Adds Back Impeachment Language to Label on Trump Portrait
item.description: The language had been removed from wall text in the National Portrait Gallery, but it’s back as the museum unveiled changes to its exhibition on U.S. presidents.
item.dc:creator: Zachary Small and Deborah Lynn Blumberg
item.media:credit: Valerie Plesch for The New York Times
item.media:description: The wall text accompanying President Trump’s image in the National Portrait Gallery has been adjusted again.

item.title: Washington National Opera Builds a New Chapter After Kennedy Center
item.description: The company announced that its next season would include a slate of five full-length operas at houses around the Washington area.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nagourney
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts

item.title: ‘In the Grey’ Review: Cavill and Gyllenhaal Bring the Ruckus
item.description: Guy Ritchie’s latest is a sleek, sun-drenched actioner in which morality is fuzzy but the fashion is sharp.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Kenny

item.title: Replacing a Royal Hand-Me-Down That Finally Frayed
item.description: Three years after King Charles III was crowned, the Royal Opera House in London hung plush new stage curtains bearing the monarch’s insignia — but only after the old ones gave up the ghost.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.category: Charles III, King of the United Kingdom
item.media:credit: Jeremie Souteyrat for The New York Times

item.title: How to Watch the Eurovision Song Contest Final
item.description: The internationally popular event will be available to U.S. viewers, too, although not on domestic TV in some countries that are boycotting this year.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Vincentelli
item.media:credit: Ilvy Njiokiktjien for The New York Times
item.media:description: Watching the Eurovision Song Contest at the press center in Rotterdam, the Netherlands, in 2021. The country is boycotting this year’s event.

item.title: 5 Children’s Movies to Stream Now
item.description: This month’s picks include an animated critter voiced by Michael B. Jordan and a nature documentary about the orangutans of Southeast Asia.
item.dc:creator: Dina Gachman
item.category: The SpongeBob Movie: Search for SquarePants (Movie)

item.title: ‘Been Here Stay Here’ Documentary Examines Tangier Island
item.description: “Been Here Stay Here” examines the complicated Chesapeake Bay dynamic when many residents don’t believe in man-made climate change.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson

item.title: ‘New Born’ Review: Let Hugh Jackman Tell You a Story
item.description: The actor gives a remarkable performance in a program of monologues at the intimate Minetta Lane Theater. Sepideh Moafi and Marianna Gailus also star.
item.dc:creator: Laura Collins-Hughes
item.media:description: Hugh Jackman in “New Born” at the Minetta Lane Theater in Manhattan.

item.title: The Soprano Rainelle Krause's Death is a Loss of More Than Just a Role
item.description: The coloratura soprano Rainelle Krause, who died in March, was one of the most sought after performers of Queen of the Night in ‘The Magic Flute.’
item.dc:creator: David Belcher

item.title: Jack Douglas, Producer for Aerosmith and Lennon, Dies at 80
item.description: A onetime Beatlemaniac, he helped shape the sound of hits like “Walk This Way” and “(Just Like) Starting Over.”
item.dc:creator: Alex Williams
item.media:description: Jack Douglas, center, the producer of Aerosmith’s 1977 album “Draw the Line,” during a recording session with the engineer Jay Messina, left, and Steven Tyler, the band’s lead singer.

item.title: Claudine Longet, Entertainer Who Shot Olympian Boyfriend, Dies at 84
item.description: A singer and actress, she drew wide attention for the fatal 1976 shooting of Spider Sabich. She was convicted of negligent homicide.
item.dc:creator: Trip Gabriel

item.title: Five Action Movies to Stream Now
item.description: This month’s picks include brawling teenagers, vengeful widowers and retired swordsmen.
item.dc:creator: Robert Daniels
item.media:description: Liu Fengchao in “The Butcher’s Blade.”

item.title: 6 New Movies Our Critics Are Talking About This Week
item.description: Whether you’re a casual moviegoer or an avid buff, our reviewers think these films are worth knowing about.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: Five Free Movies to Stream Now
item.description: This month’s collection, including Richard Linklater’s “Before” trilogy, heralds the blooming of love, and all the turmoil that comes with it.
item.dc:creator: Brandon Yu

item.title: How Meryl Streep Delivers Criticism in ‘The Devil Wears Prada 2’
item.description: The director David Frankel narrates a comedic scene featuring Streep and Anne Hathaway.
item.dc:creator: Mekado Murphy

item.title: Free Summer Concerts, Festivals and Events in NYC
item.description: In need of good times that don’t cost a dime? You’re in luck: As the weather heats up, the opportunities to have free fun are everywhere in the city. Here are some of our favorites.
item.dc:creator: Danielle Dowling
item.category: Lincoln Center for the Performing Arts

item.title: Speculative Fiction Books Full of Real Horrors
item.description: The best-selling author Vaishnavi Patel recommends speculative fiction by N.K. Jemisin, Olivie Blake and more that speaks to our world today.
item.dc:creator: Vaishnavi Patel

item.title: ‘The Devil Wears Prada 2’ | Anatomy of a Scene
item.description: The director David Frankel narrates a sequence from his film starring Meryl Streep and Anne Hathaway.
item.dc:creator: Mekado Murphy

item.title: Late Night Pokes Fun at Trump in China
item.description: Jimmy Fallon joked that President Trump’s idea of a Temple of Heaven is very different from the one he toured in Beijing with China’s leader.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)
Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)
Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)

item.title: Frank Stack, Painter Who Secretly Drew ‘The Adventures of Jesus,’ Dies at 88
item.description: For 20 years, he hid his identity behind the nom de plume Foolbert Sturgeon as he chronicled Christ’s encounters with modern-day hypocrites in comic-book form.
item.dc:creator: Michael S. Rosenwald

item.title: Clarence Carter, Singer of Lust-Filled Soul Hits, Dies at 90
item.description: In songs like “Slip Away” and “Back Door Santa,” he performed with the fervor of a backwoods preacher and the bawdy humor of a juke joint.
item.dc:creator: Bill Friskics-Warren
item.media:description: Clarence Carter in 1975. Blind from youth, he had a deep, declamatory baritone and lecherous, full-throated laugh.

item.title: Rose Byrne and Kelli O’Hara in ‘Fallen Angels,’ and More Theater to Stream
item.description: The actresses both scored Tony nominations for their hilarious turns in Noël Coward’s 1925 farce. Also catch Ben Platt and Rachel Zegler in “The Last Five Years.”
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Vincentelli
item.media:description: Rose Byrne, left, and Kelli O’Hara in “Fallen Angels” at the Todd Haines Theater in New York.

item.title: Six Unforgettable Artists at the Biggest Independent Fair
item.description: A dependably stylish fair expands and reappears on the far side of Chinatown.
item.dc:creator: Will Heinrich

item.title: At TEFAF New York, Lesser-Known Artists Delight
item.description: At the Park Avenue Armory, home to the art fair with the deepest roots in Europe, five names wooed our critic, cutting through the star system.
item.dc:creator: Walker Mimms

item.title: ‘Is God Is’ Review: The Fires This Time
item.description: Kara Young and Mallori Johnson play twins bent on revenge in the playwright Aleshea Harris’s powerhouse film debut.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Kennedy

item.title: Judith Barnard, of Best-Selling ‘Judith Michael’ Fame, Dies at 94
item.description: At 50, on a lark, she published a romance novel with her husband, Michael Fain. Like their characters, they found their lives transformed by unexpected success.
item.dc:creator: Alex Williams
item.media:description: Judith Barnard in 1999 with her husband and collaborator, Michael Fain. The couple published 11 novels, starting with “Deceptions,” an out-of-nowhere hit, in 1982.

item.title: The Met Will Expand by Merging With the Nearby Neue Galerie
item.description: Beginning in 2028, the Metropolitan Museum of Art will own the Neue’s Fifth Avenue home and the prestige collection of 20th-century Austrian and German art built by Ronald S. Lauder.
item.dc:creator: Robin Pogrebin

item.title: Who Owns These Artworks? Musée d’Orsay Hopes Visitors Can Help Find Out.
item.description: A new room in the Musée d’Orsay’s permanent display includes 13 pieces that were recovered from Germany and Austria after World War II and whose provenance is unknown.
item.dc:creator: Ségolène Le Stradic

item.title: In Venice, the Passion of Life and the Ghost of Art
item.description: The 2026 edition of the world’s oldest art exhibition is a celebration of vitality — for better and indeed for worse.
item.dc:creator: Jason Farago
item.media:credit: Matteo de Mayda for The New York Times

item.title: Beyoncé and Taylor Swift Join National Recording Registry
item.description: The collection at the Library of Congress also added works by Weezer and Vince Gill, and a radio broadcast of a fight between Muhammad Ali and Joe Frazier.
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor
item.media:credit: Chandan Khannalisa O'Connor/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Taylor Swift’s album “1989” is entering the National Recording Registry, along with Beyoncé’s “Single Ladies” and 23 other works.

item.title: Julieta Venegas, a Mexican Pop Hitmaker, Is Looking Homeward
item.description: On her new album, “Norteña,” the singer embraces regional traditions and unlocks her most personal songwriting yet.
item.dc:creator: Jon Pareles
item.media:credit: Amanda Hakan for The New York Times

item.title: Best Booths at Frieze, the Workhorse of Contemporary Art
item.description: Among the 65 galleries at the Shed are paintings, paintings, paintings, and a surprising trove of small sculptures.
item.dc:creator: Will Heinrich
item.media:credit: Lila Barth for The New York Times
item.media:description: A crowd at the Frieze New York Fair reflected in “Orbit Dance 6 O’Clock,” 2022, by Ayoung Kim in the Gallery Hyundai booth.

item.title: How an Image of Washington at Prayer Became a Touchstone for the Right
item.description: A scene inspired by the winter at Valley Forge has become more prominent in the Trump era, along with claims that the United States was founded as a Christian nation.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Schuessler

item.title: How the Stars of 6 Tony-Nominated Broadway Shows Get Into Character
item.description: For Nathan Lane, Taraji P. Henson, Daniel Radcliffe and other actors, it can take wigs, group hugs or banishing ghosts to make the transformation.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson, George Etheredge, Amir Hamja and Vincent Tullo
item.media:credit: George Etheredge for The New York Times

item.title: ‘Obsession’ Review: I Love You to Death
item.description: Passion becomes possession in Curry Barker’s supernatural, be-careful-what-you-wish-for horror movie.
item.dc:creator: Erik Piepenburg
item.media:description: Inde Navarrette, left, with Michael Johnston in “Obsession.”

item.title: ‘Magic Hour’ Review: Grief Encounters
item.description: Katie Aselton’s melodrama follows a woman in the California desert who is reeling after a mysterious marriage conflict.
item.dc:creator: Natalia Winkelman

item.title: ‘Life Hack’ Review: A Heist That Never Leaves the Screen
item.description: A group of teenagers seem to be targeting a cryptocurrency billionaire out of boredom, but they have a more sympathetic motive in this hacker film.
item.dc:creator: Chris Azzopardi

item.title: ‘Decorado’ Review: Cute Mice Meet Cold Orwellian Despair
item.description: This surreal satire blends anthropomorphic charm with existential dread to critique capitalism, though its meta-commentary leaves some haunting questions frustratingly unanswered.
item.dc:creator: Maya Phillips
item.media:description: A scene from “Decorado.”

item.title: ‘Agatha’s Almanac’ Review: Living Off the Land
item.description: Over six years, a filmmaker captured her aunt’s largely self-sufficient lifestyle and eccentricities on a 54-acre farm in Canada.
item.dc:creator: Ben Kenigsberg

item.title: The Villains Who Shaped ‘Survivor’
item.description: From Richard Hatch to Russell Hantz and beyond, the landmark reality show brought a new type of antagonist to TV screens.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Abrams

item.title: ‘The Wizard of the Kremlin’ Review: The New Rasputin
item.description: Paul Dano and Jude Law star in a movie about the rise of Vladimir Putin and a fictional version of his right-hand man.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson

item.title: Stephen Colbert Slams ‘Trump’s Fabulous Billionaire Boys Trip’
item.description: Late night hosts responded to President Trump’s arrival in Beijing on Wednesday, accompanied by 17 chief executives including Tim Cook and Elon Musk.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)
Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)
The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)

item.title: Shakira, Madonna and BTS Are First World Cup Final Halftime Show
item.description: The previous World Cup final drew more than 500 million live viewers. This year’s matchup is scheduled for July 19 at MetLife Stadium.
item.dc:creator: Emmanuel Morgan
item.media:description: Shakira recorded the official World Cup song, “Dai Dai,” which features the Afrobeats star Burna Boy.

item.title: Ye Must Pay Musicians for Using Sample Without Permission
item.description: A jury in Los Angeles found the rapper and three of his companies liable for more than $400,000 after he played an unauthorized music sample at an event in 2021.
item.dc:creator: Michaela Towfighi
item.media:description: A spokesman for Ye called the verdict a “failed shakedown.”

item.title: Cynthia Shange, Who Defied Apartheid at a Beauty Pageant, Dies at 76
item.description: She was the first Black South African to enter the Miss World contest, placing in the top five in 1972.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nossiter
item.media:description: Cynthia Shange competing at a Miss World event in London in 1972. South Africa’s president praised the boldness of her ambitions “in an era when apartheid segregation was at its peak.”

item.title: Koji Suzuki, Whose Horror Novels Inspired ‘Ring’ Movies, Dies at 68
item.description: Sometimes called the Stephen King of Japan, he helped create a genre known as J-horror and spawned one of the highest-grossing horror films ever made.
item.dc:creator: Jeré Longman
item.media:credit: Jennifer S. Altman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Koji Suzuki in 2004. Although his horror fiction made him wealthy, he was not a fan of the genre. “I really dislike most horror writing,” he told The New York Times.

item.title: The Good List: 6 Things to Add Joy to Your Day
item.description: Voice memos, snail mail and your own private screening room.
item.dc:creator: Melissa Kirsch

item.title: Addiction Counselor Who Helped Supply Ketamine to Matthew Perry Sentenced to Two Years
item.description: Erik Fleming, a licensed drug addiction counselor, had admitted to selling 51 vials of ketamine to the actor, including the dose that killed him.
item.dc:creator: Matt Stevens
item.media:description: Matthew Perry, who played Chandler Bing on the 1990s sitcom “Friends,” publicly struggled with drug and alcohol addiction for decades.

item.title: At Cannes 2026, Gaza Becomes a Political Flashpoint
item.description: The event’s leaders immediately jumped into the debate over whether festival participants should speak out on issues like Israel and Gaza.
item.dc:creator: Kyle Buchanan
item.media:description: From left, the Cannes Film Festival president, Iris Knobloch, the jury president, Peter Jackson, and the festival director, Thierry Frémaux.

item.title: Mel Brooks Donates His Archives to the National Comedy Center
item.description: The comedian is contributing some 20,000 documents and photos covering his career, including his time in the Army and the lyrics for “Springtime for Hitler.”
item.dc:creator: Jason Zinoman

item.title: Can Three Auction Houses Sell $2.6 Billion Worth of Art in One Week?
item.description: Five luxury artworks hold the key to the spring season, one of the most anticipated sales in years. Major buyers are looking past female and younger artists and toward tradition.
item.dc:creator: Tim F. Schneider, Julia Halperin and Zachary Small

item.title: ‘A New Leaf’: A Romantic Comedy, With Murder on the Mind
item.description: This 1971 movie, restored and revived for a week at the IFC Center, offers ample evidence of Elaine May’s screwball genius.
item.dc:creator: J. Hoberman

item.title: Cy Twombly, From Intimate Angles
item.description: In photos by his wife, Tatiana Franchetti, whose negatives were discovered by his granddaughter, we see more personal sides to the 20th-century master.
item.dc:creator: Arthur Lubow

item.title: ‘They Shooting at Us’: Rihanna and ASAP Rocky Outlined Attack to Police
item.description: The singers discussed their stalkers and their experience inside a trailer struck by gunfire, according to a police report. A woman has been charged with attempted murder.
item.dc:creator: Matt Stevens
item.media:credit: Nina Westervelt for The New York Times
item.media:description: Rihanna and ASAP Rocky at the Met Gala last week, about two months after a shooting at her home.

item.title: ‘Ragtime’ Star Joshua Henry Is Bringing Broadway to Its Feet
item.description: Joshua Henry, a Tony nominee for his thrilling performance in the musical, credits the demanding role with helping him “feel like I have mastered the voice.”
item.dc:creator: Brittani Samuel and Vincent Tullo

item.title: ‘The A List’ Review: The Diaspora, Described
item.description: This documentary spotlights the individual histories and struggles of 15 figures from all walks of life, connected by their inclusion in the Asian and Pacific diasporas.
item.dc:creator: Brandon Yu
item.category: The A List: 15 Stories From Asian and Pacific Diasporas (Movie)
